observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete physical count of PPE and submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant, and Equipment (RPCPPE) to the Office of the Auditor not later than January 31 of each year; and
the MGSO Designate and Municipal Accountant to maintain and regularly update the Property and Ledger Cards, ensuring that these records are reconciled with the actual physical count to establish the reliability of the balances of PPE accounts presented in the financial statements.
Additionally, we recommended that the Municipality avail of the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts as provided in COA Circular 
No. 2020-006 during the next physical count of Property, Plant, and Equipment to accurately determine and establish the correct PPE balance.

We recommended, and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Acting Municipal Accountant to:
Require the Municipal Accountant to: Recognize the cost of ongoing projects as Construction in Progress and reclassify them to the appropriate PPE account after completion; and
Prepare the necessary entries to correct the error amounting to ₱25,989.21 and transfer of PPE totaling ₱36.919 million to the General Fund and related depreciation.
We recommended to identify projects implemented through fund transfers from prior years and record the corresponding asset transfers to the General Fund and related depreciation.

We recommended, and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Recognize and record accumulated depreciation for all existing Property, Plant, and Equipment (PPE) accounts following IPSAS;
Compute and properly apply the correct recognition of depreciation expense for PPE accounts for the current year; and
Make the necessary prior period adjustments to accurately reflect their balances in the year-end financial statements.
We also recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to consider hiring personnel with an accounting background to be assigned to the Accounting Department, to address the staffing shortage that contributed to the deficiencies noted in the audit.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to:
Require the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to provide the Acting-Municipal Accountant at the beginning of the year with the duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to serve as the basis for the set-up of the correct RPT/SET Receivables in the books; and
Direct the Acting-Municipal Accountant to review the recording of receivables and reconcile the accounting records with the Municipal Treasurer’s reports and immediately effect the necessary corrections and adjustments.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to:
Prioritize the reconciliation of the ₱10.547 million discrepancy by thoroughly reviewing the accounting records, cashbooks, and supporting documentation to identify and promptly resolve variances;
Conduct a comprehensive review of all financial transactions for the current year to prevent discrepancies in the future and to strengthen the reliability of the Municipality’s financial reporting; and
Establish and implement robust internal controls for monitoring cash balances, ensuring monthly reconciliation between the General Ledger and Cash book, and regularly verifying the accuracy of financial data.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer's Office (MTO) to explore all feasible methods to improve collection efforts, aiming to meet the targets set by the Bureau of Local Government Finance (BLGF).
We also recommended the full adherence to the posting guidelines for the Notice of Delinquencies.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct concerned officials/personnel to:
Immediately submit the lacking supporting documents for the travel claims already paid to Job Order personnel, particularly the certification from the Head of the Agency or any authorized approving official, in compliance with the requirements set forth under COA Resolution No. 2021-044.

Strengthen internal controls and implement measures to guarantee that disbursements are properly documented, authorized, and compliant with applicable rules and regulations.
